responses.content: The central idea about female empowerment in “ Hatshepsut: His Majesty, Herself “ is that women can achieve positions of power by adapting to male  dominate system ,which contracts with the idea in “ Elena “ where female empowerment is a personal journey of resilience and determination to achieve self-improvement and benefit her family.

questions.question:
What central idea does the author of Hatshepsut: His Majesty, Herself convey about female empowerment? How does this idea compare and contrast with that of “Elena”? 


Respond to this question in the form of a paragraph that includes a claim, at least two pieces of evidence (one from each text), and an explanation of how your evidence supports your claim.

responses.content: His Majesty, Herself , Catherine Andronick  conveys that female empowerment involves defying traditional gender roles and creating one’s own path to authority. Hatshepsut’s exemplified this by adopting the title of “pharaoh “a traditionally male role  and even wearing masculine regalia such as a false beard to legitimize her power.

His Majesty, Herself , Catherine Andronick  conveys that female empowerment involves defying traditional gender roles and creating one’s own path to authority. Hatshepsut’s exemplified this by adopting the title of “pharaoh “a traditionally male role  and even wearing masculine regalia such as a false beard to legitimize her power.This idea of personal agency is echoed in the pat Moras poem “ Elena”, where the protagonist also seeks to empower herself by breaking through societal and language barriers. While Hatshepsut assert herself in a public , political sphere , Elena’s empowerment is more personal and domestic, as she strives to learn English to better connect with her children. Despite  these different s contexts , both woman demonstrate a shared central idea: empowerment is achieved through resilience and the determination to overcome limitations imposed by their society’s.

Hatshepsut’s exemplified this by adopting the title of “pharaoh “a traditionally male role  and even wearing masculine regalia such as a false beard to legitimize her power.This idea of personal agency is echoed in the pat Moras poem “ Elena”, where the protagonist also seeks to empower herself by breaking through societal and language barriers. While Hatshepsut assert herself in a public , political sphere , Elena’s empowerment is more personal and domestic, as she strives to learn English to better connect with her children. Despite  these different s contexts , both woman demonstrate a shared central idea: empowerment is achieved through resilience and the determination to overcome limitations imposed by their society’s.
